Correction: The second example shows the tradeoff more clearly. Concurrency is about progress on multiple tasks, not necessarily simultaneous CPU execution.

Trade-offs & When to Avoid

Do not pick a concurrency tool because it sounds advanced. Start with the workload shape, then choose the minimum model that matches it.

Pitfalls & Gotchas

A common trap around Concurrency vs Parallelism vs Asynchrony
1import time, concurrent.futures2 3def cpu_work(n):4    # Purely CPU-bound: count to n5    total = 06    for i in range(n):7        total += i * i8    return total9 10N = 500_00011 12# Single process: one core does everything sequentially13t0 = time.perf_counter()14cpu_work(N); cpu_work(N)15seq = time.perf_counter() - t016 17# ProcessPoolExecutor: each worker gets its own interpreter -> true parallelism18t0 = time.perf_counter()19with concurrent.futures.ProcessPoolExecutor(max_workers=2) as ex:20    list(ex.map(cpu_work, [N, N]))21par = time.perf_counter() - t022 23print(f"Sequential : {seq:.3f}s")24print(f"Parallel   : {par:.3f}s  <- faster on multi-core for CPU work")25print("GIL prevents thread-level CPU parallelism; processes bypass it")
Output
PermissionError: [WinError 5] Access is denied

Root cause: The bug comes from assuming the shortest form is always the clearest or safest form. In Python, the runtime model still matters.

Fix: Separate processes avoid the single-CPython-interpreter-thread bottleneck for CPU-bound pure-Python code.

Self-Check Questions

What problem does Concurrency vs Parallelism vs Asynchrony solve?
These distinctions matter because using the wrong concurrency model can make code slower, harder to debug, or both.
What mental model should you picture for Concurrency vs Parallelism vs Asynchrony?
How CPU-bound and I/O-bound workloads differ, Why threads, processes, and event loops solve different problems, How to choose the right model before writing code
When should you limit or avoid Concurrency vs Parallelism vs Asynchrony?
Do not pick a concurrency tool because it sounds advanced. Start with the workload shape, then choose the minimum model that matches it.
Back to Dependency TableNext concept → PY46
PY46 · Layer 10 · Concurrency & Parallelism

Threading

Read firstPY33PY34
Used later inPY54PY55PY56PY57PY58PY59

What you'll learn

  • What the GIL protects and what it does not
  • Why threads are still useful for I/O-bound work
  • How locks and coordination primitives avoid races
Why This Matters
Threads exist because overlapping waiting is valuable, and many operating-system or C-extension operations release the GIL while work is in progress.

Concept Explanation

Threads let one process make progress on multiple tasks concurrently, but in CPython the GIL means CPU-bound pure-Python threads do not execute Python bytecode in parallel.

Threads exist because overlapping waiting is valuable, and many operating-system or C-extension operations release the GIL while work is in progress.

Builds on: PY33, PY34. Prepares you for: PY54, PY55, PY56.

Mental Model

one process -> multiple threads share memory -> GIL coordinates bytecode execution -> synchronization still needed for shared state

Worked Examples

Example 1. Threads still help with waiting

What this does and why: The GIL does not stop overlap on blocking I/O because threads can switch while one is waiting.

1import threading2import time3 4def task(name):5    time.sleep(0.2)6    print(name)7 8threads = [threading.Thread(target=task, args=(f"t{i}",)) for i in range(3)]9[t.start() for t in threads]10[t.join() for t in threads]
Output
t2
t0
t1

What to observe: The sleeps overlap, so total elapsed time is closer to one sleep than to three added together.

Example 2. A race condition on shared state

What this does and why: Shared mutable state is the danger zone for threads.

1import threading2 3counter = 04lock = threading.Lock()5with lock:6    counter += 17print(counter)
Output
1

What to observe: The example shows the fix shape: protect the critical section with a lock. Without synchronization, repeated updates can interleave unpredictably.

Example 3. Other coordination primitives

What this does and why: Threads need more than one tool depending on the coordination pattern.

1import threading2print(threading.RLock)3print(threading.Semaphore)4print(threading.Event)5print(threading.Condition)
Output
<function RLock at 0x000002A7AEE24900>
<class 'threading.Semaphore'>
<class 'threading.Event'>
<class 'threading.Condition'>

What to observe: Use `Lock` for mutual exclusion, `RLock` for re-entrant locking, `Semaphore` to cap concurrency, `Event` for one-bit signaling, and `Condition` for coordinated waiting.

Common Misconceptions

Misconception

Wrong assumption: Threading is only syntax sugar with no runtime model behind it.

1import threading2import time3 4def task(name):5    time.sleep(0.2)6    print(name)7 8threads = [threading.Thread(target=task, args=(f"t{i}",)) for i in range(3)]9[t.start() for t in threads]10[t.join() for t in threads]
Output
t2
t0
t1

Correction: Run the first example and watch the behavior directly. The sleeps overlap, so total elapsed time is closer to one sleep than to three added together.

Misconception

Wrong assumption: Once the common case for Threading works, the edge cases no longer matter.

1import threading2 3counter = 04lock = threading.Lock()5with lock:6    counter += 17print(counter)
Output
1

Correction: The second example shows the tradeoff more clearly. The example shows the fix shape: protect the critical section with a lock. Without synchronization, repeated updates can interleave unpredictably.

Trade-offs & When to Avoid

Threads are often the simplest fix for I/O overlap, but once shared-state complexity dominates, a queue-based or process-based design may be easier to reason about.

Pitfalls & Gotchas

A common trap around Threading
1import threading2print(threading.RLock)3print(threading.Semaphore)4print(threading.Event)5print(threading.Condition)
Output
<function RLock at 0x000002A7AEE24900>
<class 'threading.Semaphore'>
<class 'threading.Event'>
<class 'threading.Condition'>

Root cause: The bug comes from assuming the shortest form is always the clearest or safest form. In Python, the runtime model still matters.

Fix: Use `Lock` for mutual exclusion, `RLock` for re-entrant locking, `Semaphore` to cap concurrency, `Event` for one-bit signaling, and `Condition` for coordinated waiting.

Self-Check Questions

What problem does Threading solve?
Threads exist because overlapping waiting is valuable, and many operating-system or C-extension operations release the GIL while work is in progress.
What mental model should you picture for Threading?
What the GIL protects and what it does not, Why threads are still useful for I/O-bound work, How locks and coordination primitives avoid races
When should you limit or avoid Threading?
Threads are often the simplest fix for I/O overlap, but once shared-state complexity dominates, a queue-based or process-based design may be easier to reason about.
Back to Dependency TableNext concept → PY47
PY47 · Layer 10 · Concurrency & Parallelism

Multiprocessing

Read firstPY33PY34
Used later inPY54PY55PY56PY57PY58PY59

What you'll learn

  • Why separate processes bypass the GIL
  • How pools, queues, pipes, and shared memory differ
  • Why picklability matters when sending work to workers
Why This Matters
Separate processes matter because CPU-bound Python work often needs more than concurrency; it needs multiple interpreters running at once.

Concept Explanation

Multiprocessing uses separate processes to achieve real CPU parallelism for pure-Python code, at the cost of separate memory spaces and serialization overhead.

Separate processes matter because CPU-bound Python work often needs more than concurrency; it needs multiple interpreters running at once.

Builds on: PY33, PY34. Prepares you for: PY54, PY55, PY56.

Mental Model

parent process -> worker processes -> tasks serialized to workers -> results serialized back

Worked Examples

Example 1. A process pool maps work across inputs

What this does and why: Pools are the high-level entry point for many CPU-bound batch jobs.

1from multiprocessing import Pool2 3def square(n):4    return n * n5 6if __name__ == "__main__":7    with Pool() as pool:8        print(pool.map(square, [1, 2, 3, 4]))
Output
PermissionError: [WinError 5] Access is denied

What to observe: The guard is required on some platforms because worker processes import the module to start.

Example 2. Queue versus pipe

What this does and why: Both move data between processes, but they serve slightly different communication shapes.

1from multiprocessing import Pipe, Queue2print(Queue)3print(Pipe)
Output
<bound method BaseContext.Queue of <multiprocessing.context.DefaultContext object at 0x00000222A1FE5610>>
<bound method BaseContext.Pipe of <multiprocessing.context.DefaultContext object at 0x00000222A1FE5610>>

What to observe: Use queues for general producer-consumer patterns and pipes for simpler two-endpoint communication.

Example 3. Arguments must usually be picklable

What this does and why: Worker processes need a serialized form of the task and its data.

1import pickle2 3# Top-level functions pickle fine4import math5data = pickle.dumps(math.sqrt)6print("top-level function pickles:", len(data), "bytes")7 8# Lambda cannot be pickled9try:10    pickle.dumps(lambda x: x)11except AttributeError as exc:12    print("lambda fails pickle:", exc)13 14# Plain data pickles fine15payload = pickle.dumps({"task": "square", "values": [1, 2, 3]})16restored = pickle.loads(payload)17print("dict round-trip:", restored)18print("Use top-level functions and plain data as multiprocessing arguments")
Output
top-level function pickles: 28 bytes
PicklingError: Can't pickle <function <lambda> at 0x000001D6AA09A5C0>: attribute lookup <lambda> on __main__ failed

What to observe: If the work item cannot be pickled, the child process cannot reconstruct it. That is a common source of multiprocessing errors.

Common Misconceptions

Misconception

Wrong assumption: Multiprocessing is only syntax sugar with no runtime model behind it.

1from multiprocessing import Pool2 3def square(n):4    return n * n5 6if __name__ == "__main__":7    with Pool() as pool:8        print(pool.map(square, [1, 2, 3, 4]))
Output
PermissionError: [WinError 5] Access is denied

Correction: Run the first example and watch the behavior directly. The guard is required on some platforms because worker processes import the module to start.

Misconception

Wrong assumption: Once the common case for Multiprocessing works, the edge cases no longer matter.

1from multiprocessing import Pipe, Queue2print(Queue)3print(Pipe)
Output
<bound method BaseContext.Queue of <multiprocessing.context.DefaultContext object at 0x00000222A1FE5610>>
<bound method BaseContext.Pipe of <multiprocessing.context.DefaultContext object at 0x00000222A1FE5610>>

Correction: The second example shows the tradeoff more clearly. Use queues for general producer-consumer patterns and pipes for simpler two-endpoint communication.

Trade-offs & When to Avoid

Processes buy true parallelism, but startup cost, memory duplication, and serialization overhead mean they are not the answer to every workload.

Pitfalls & Gotchas

A common trap around Multiprocessing
1import pickle2 3# Top-level functions pickle fine4import math5data = pickle.dumps(math.sqrt)6print("top-level function pickles:", len(data), "bytes")7 8# Lambda cannot be pickled9try:10    pickle.dumps(lambda x: x)11except AttributeError as exc:12    print("lambda fails pickle:", exc)13 14# Plain data pickles fine15payload = pickle.dumps({"task": "square", "values": [1, 2, 3]})16restored = pickle.loads(payload)17print("dict round-trip:", restored)18print("Use top-level functions and plain data as multiprocessing arguments")
Output
top-level function pickles: 28 bytes
PicklingError: Can't pickle <function <lambda> at 0x000001D6AA09A5C0>: attribute lookup <lambda> on __main__ failed

Root cause: The bug comes from assuming the shortest form is always the clearest or safest form. In Python, the runtime model still matters.

Fix: If the work item cannot be pickled, the child process cannot reconstruct it. That is a common source of multiprocessing errors.

Self-Check Questions

What problem does Multiprocessing solve?
Separate processes matter because CPU-bound Python work often needs more than concurrency; it needs multiple interpreters running at once.
What mental model should you picture for Multiprocessing?
Why separate processes bypass the GIL, How pools, queues, pipes, and shared memory differ, Why picklability matters when sending work to workers
When should you limit or avoid Multiprocessing?
Processes buy true parallelism, but startup cost, memory duplication, and serialization overhead mean they are not the answer to every workload.
Back to Dependency TableNext concept → PY48
PY48 · Layer 10 · Concurrency & Parallelism

asyncio

Read firstPY33PY34
Used later inPY54PY55PY56PY57PY58PY59

What you'll learn

  • How the event loop schedules coroutines
  • Why blocking the loop is the cardinal async mistake
  • When `gather`, `wait`, and `TaskGroup` differ
Why This Matters
Async I/O exists because one thread can often manage many waiting network tasks efficiently if code cooperates instead of blocking.

Concept Explanation

`asyncio` provides cooperative concurrency with an event loop, coroutines, tasks, and non-blocking I/O-friendly APIs.

Async I/O exists because one thread can often manage many waiting network tasks efficiently if code cooperates instead of blocking.

Builds on: PY33, PY34. Prepares you for: PY54, PY55, PY56.

Mental Model

event loop -> ready task queue + I/O readiness notifications -> resume suspended coroutines

Worked Examples

Example 1. Basic coroutine execution

What this does and why: `async def` defines a coroutine function, and `asyncio.run` creates and drives the event loop for a top-level entry point.

1import asyncio2 3async def greet():4    await asyncio.sleep(0.1)5    return "hello"6 7print(asyncio.run(greet()))
Output
hello

What to observe: The `await` gives control back to the loop while the sleep is pending.

Example 2. Run several coroutines together

What this does and why: `gather` waits for a group of awaitables and returns their results in input order.

1import asyncio2 3async def fetch(name):4    await asyncio.sleep(0.1)5    return name6 7async def main():8    print(await asyncio.gather(fetch("a"), fetch("b")))9 10asyncio.run(main())
Output
['a', 'b']

What to observe: `TaskGroup` in Python 3.11+ offers stronger structured-concurrency semantics for related tasks.

Example 3. Do not block the event loop

What this does and why: A blocking call freezes every coroutine sharing the loop until it finishes.

1import asyncio2import time3 4async def bad():5    time.sleep(0.2)6    return "blocked loop"7 8print(asyncio.run(bad()))
Output
blocked loop

What to observe: Replace blocking work with non-blocking APIs or move it to a thread or executor so other tasks can keep progressing.

Common Misconceptions

Misconception

Wrong assumption: asyncio is only syntax sugar with no runtime model behind it.

1import asyncio2 3async def greet():4    await asyncio.sleep(0.1)5    return "hello"6 7print(asyncio.run(greet()))
Output
hello

Correction: Run the first example and watch the behavior directly. The `await` gives control back to the loop while the sleep is pending.

Misconception

Wrong assumption: Once the common case for asyncio works, the edge cases no longer matter.

1import asyncio2 3async def fetch(name):4    await asyncio.sleep(0.1)5    return name6 7async def main():8    print(await asyncio.gather(fetch("a"), fetch("b")))9 10asyncio.run(main())
Output
['a', 'b']

Correction: The second example shows the tradeoff more clearly. `TaskGroup` in Python 3.11+ offers stronger structured-concurrency semantics for related tasks.

Trade-offs & When to Avoid

Async code is not automatically faster. It shines when many tasks spend time waiting on network or other async-compatible I/O.

Pitfalls & Gotchas

A common trap around asyncio
1import asyncio2import time3 4async def bad():5    time.sleep(0.2)6    return "blocked loop"7 8print(asyncio.run(bad()))
Output
blocked loop

Root cause: The bug comes from assuming the shortest form is always the clearest or safest form. In Python, the runtime model still matters.

Fix: Replace blocking work with non-blocking APIs or move it to a thread or executor so other tasks can keep progressing.

Self-Check Questions

What problem does asyncio solve?
Async I/O exists because one thread can often manage many waiting network tasks efficiently if code cooperates instead of blocking.
What mental model should you picture for asyncio?
How the event loop schedules coroutines, Why blocking the loop is the cardinal async mistake, When `gather`, `wait`, and `TaskGroup` differ
When should you limit or avoid asyncio?
Async code is not automatically faster. It shines when many tasks spend time waiting on network or other async-compatible I/O.
Back to Dependency TableNext concept → PY49
PY49 · Layer 11 · Type System & Tooling

Type Hints

Read firstPY06PY30
Used later inPY54PY55PY56PY57PY58PY59

What you'll learn

  • How basic annotations, unions, literals, generics, and protocols fit together
  • Why type variables and parameter specs matter for reusable APIs
  • How hints improve tooling without changing runtime semantics by themselves
Why This Matters
Large codebases need machine-checkable documentation about data flow. Type hints provide that without giving up Python's dynamic runtime.

Concept Explanation

Type hints describe expected shapes of values for humans and static analyzers, even though Python itself does not enforce most annotations at runtime.

Large codebases need machine-checkable documentation about data flow. Type hints provide that without giving up Python's dynamic runtime.

Builds on: PY06, PY30. Prepares you for: PY54, PY55, PY56.

Mental Model

annotation syntax -> metadata attached to function or variable -> static tool reads it later

Worked Examples

Example 1. Basic function and variable annotations

What this does and why: Annotations make intent explicit at the API boundary.

1from typing import Optional2 3user_id: int = 74 5def find_name(user_id: int) -> Optional[str]:6    return "Ada" if user_id == 7 else None7 8print(user_id)9print(find_name(user_id))10print(find_name(0))
Output
7
Ada
None

What to observe: `Optional[str]` means the function may return `str` or `None`.

Example 2. Generics with `TypeVar`

What this does and why: Generic code can stay precise without hard-coding one concrete type.

1from typing import TypeVar2 3T = TypeVar("T")4 5def first(items: list[T]) -> T:6    return items[0]7 8print(first([10, 20, 30]))9print(first(["a", "b", "c"]))
Output
10
a

What to observe: The type variable says the return type matches the element type of the input list.

Example 3. Advanced hints for decorators and value restrictions

What this does and why: `Literal`, `Annotated`, and `ParamSpec` cover narrower but important cases.

1from typing import Annotated, Literal2 3Mode = Literal["r", "w"]4Port = Annotated[int, "1..65535"]5 6print(Mode)7print(Port)
Output
typing.Literal['r', 'w']
typing.Annotated[int, '1..65535']

What to observe: These hints communicate stronger intent to tools and readers, even though plain Python will not enforce them on its own.

Common Misconceptions

Misconception

Wrong assumption: Type Hints is only syntax sugar with no runtime model behind it.

1from typing import Optional2 3user_id: int = 74 5def find_name(user_id: int) -> Optional[str]:6    return "Ada" if user_id == 7 else None7 8print(user_id)9print(find_name(user_id))10print(find_name(0))
Output
7
Ada
None

Correction: Run the first example and watch the behavior directly. `Optional[str]` means the function may return `str` or `None`.

Misconception

Wrong assumption: Once the common case for Type Hints works, the edge cases no longer matter.

1from typing import TypeVar2 3T = TypeVar("T")4 5def first(items: list[T]) -> T:6    return items[0]7 8print(first([10, 20, 30]))9print(first(["a", "b", "c"]))
Output
10
a

Correction: The second example shows the tradeoff more clearly. The type variable says the return type matches the element type of the input list.

Trade-offs & When to Avoid

Hints should clarify APIs, not drown them in ceremony. Type every detail only when the extra precision actually helps readers or tools catch mistakes.

Pitfalls & Gotchas

A common trap around Type Hints
1from typing import Annotated, Literal2 3Mode = Literal["r", "w"]4Port = Annotated[int, "1..65535"]5 6print(Mode)7print(Port)
Output
typing.Literal['r', 'w']
typing.Annotated[int, '1..65535']

Root cause: The bug comes from assuming the shortest form is always the clearest or safest form. In Python, the runtime model still matters.

Fix: These hints communicate stronger intent to tools and readers, even though plain Python will not enforce them on its own.

Self-Check Questions

What problem does Type Hints solve?
Large codebases need machine-checkable documentation about data flow. Type hints provide that without giving up Python's dynamic runtime.
What mental model should you picture for Type Hints?
How basic annotations, unions, literals, generics, and protocols fit together, Why type variables and parameter specs matter for reusable APIs, How hints improve tooling without changing runtime semantics by themselves
When should you limit or avoid Type Hints?
Hints should clarify APIs, not drown them in ceremony. Type every detail only when the extra precision actually helps readers or tools catch mistakes.
Back to Dependency TableNext concept → PY50
PY50 · Layer 11 · Type System & Tooling

Static Analysis

Read firstPY06PY30
Used later inPY54PY55PY56PY57PY58PY59

What you'll learn

  • Why type hints alone do nothing at runtime
  • How static tools catch bugs before execution
  • When mypy and pyright differ in workflow and emphasis
Why This Matters
Static analysis matters because some bugs are easier and cheaper to catch from source code shape than from production failures.

Concept Explanation

Static analyzers such as mypy and pyright read your code without running it and flag type inconsistencies, unreachable paths, and other issues earlier.

Static analysis matters because some bugs are easier and cheaper to catch from source code shape than from production failures.

Builds on: PY06, PY30. Prepares you for: PY54, PY55, PY56.

Mental Model

source + annotations -> analyzer builds model -> reports mismatches before runtime

Worked Examples

Example 1. Annotations are metadata, not runtime enforcement

What this does and why: Python will happily run code with the wrong value type unless you add your own checks or external validation.

1def add_one(value: int) -> int:2    return value + 13 4print(add_one("7"))
Output
TypeError: can only concatenate str (not "int") to str

What to observe: The code still runs until the unsupported operation triggers a runtime error. The annotation alone did not stop the call.

Example 2. A mypy configuration sketch

What this does and why: Static analysis becomes more useful when the tool settings are explicit and repeatable.

1config = """2[mypy]3python_version = 3.124warn_unused_ignores = True5disallow_untyped_defs = True6"""7print(config)
Output

[mypy]
python_version = 3.12
warn_unused_ignores = True
disallow_untyped_defs = True

What to observe: Treat the configuration as part of the project contract, just like linting or test settings.

Example 3. A hint can reveal a real bug

What this does and why: The analyzer sees that a `None` branch was not handled even if your test path did not hit it yet.

1from typing import Optional2 3def shout(name: Optional[str]) -> str:4    return name.upper()5 6print(shout("ada"))7try:8    print(shout(None))9except AttributeError as exc:10    print(type(exc).__name__, exc)
Output
ADA
AttributeError 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'upper'

What to observe: A static checker complains because `name` might be `None`. That warning points to a real bug before production users hit it.

Common Misconceptions

Misconception

Wrong assumption: Static Analysis is only syntax sugar with no runtime model behind it.

1def add_one(value: int) -> int:2    return value + 13 4print(add_one("7"))
Output
TypeError: can only concatenate str (not "int") to str

Correction: Run the first example and watch the behavior directly. The code still runs until the unsupported operation triggers a runtime error. The annotation alone did not stop the call.

Misconception

Wrong assumption: Once the common case for Static Analysis works, the edge cases no longer matter.

1config = """2[mypy]3python_version = 3.124warn_unused_ignores = True5disallow_untyped_defs = True6"""7print(config)
Output

[mypy]
python_version = 3.12
warn_unused_ignores = True
disallow_untyped_defs = True

Correction: The second example shows the tradeoff more clearly. Treat the configuration as part of the project contract, just like linting or test settings.

Trade-offs & When to Avoid

Use static analysis as an assistant, not a religion. pyright is often faster and editor-friendly; mypy remains deeply configurable and widely adopted.

Pitfalls & Gotchas

A common trap around Static Analysis
1from typing import Optional2 3def shout(name: Optional[str]) -> str:4    return name.upper()5 6print(shout("ada"))7try:8    print(shout(None))9except AttributeError as exc:10    print(type(exc).__name__, exc)
Output
ADA
AttributeError 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'upper'

Root cause: The bug comes from assuming the shortest form is always the clearest or safest form. In Python, the runtime model still matters.

Fix: A static checker complains because `name` might be `None`. That warning points to a real bug before production users hit it.

Self-Check Questions

What problem does Static Analysis solve?
Static analysis matters because some bugs are easier and cheaper to catch from source code shape than from production failures.
What mental model should you picture for Static Analysis?
Why type hints alone do nothing at runtime, How static tools catch bugs before execution, When mypy and pyright differ in workflow and emphasis
When should you limit or avoid Static Analysis?
Use static analysis as an assistant, not a religion. pyright is often faster and editor-friendly; mypy remains deeply configurable and widely adopted.
Back to Dependency TableNext concept → PY51
PY51 · Layer 12 · Testing

unittest

Read firstPY25PY35
Used later inPY54PY55PY56PY57PY58PY59

What you'll learn

  • How `TestCase` organizes tests
  • What `setUp`, `tearDown`, and class-level fixtures do
  • Why assertion methods communicate intent better than plain `assert` in this framework
Why This Matters
Tests exist so code changes can be checked automatically. `unittest` provides a standard structure that ships with Python itself.

Concept Explanation

`unittest` is Python's built-in xUnit-style testing framework with test case classes, fixtures, and many assertion helpers.

Tests exist so code changes can be checked automatically. `unittest` provides a standard structure that ships with Python itself.

Builds on: PY25, PY35. Prepares you for: PY54, PY55, PY56.

Mental Model

test runner -> discovers TestCase methods -> runs fixtures -> executes assertions -> reports failures

Worked Examples

Example 1. A `BankAccount` test case

What this does and why: This continues the class example from the OOP layer and shows the basic xUnit structure.

1import unittest2 3class BankAccount:4    def __init__(self, balance=0):5        self.balance = balance6    def deposit(self, amount):7        self.balance += amount8        return self.balance9 10class BankAccountTests(unittest.TestCase):11    def setUp(self):12        self.account = BankAccount(100)13    def test_deposit(self):14        self.assertEqual(self.account.deposit(25), 125)15 16case = BankAccountTests()17case.setUp()18case.test_deposit()19print("passed")
Output
passed

What to observe: `setUp` runs before each test method, giving each test a fresh fixture.

Example 2. Class-level fixtures

What this does and why: Use these when setup is expensive and can safely be shared by all tests in the class.

1class DemoTests(unittest.TestCase):2    @classmethod3    def setUpClass(cls):4        cls.shared = [1, 2, 3]5    @classmethod6    def tearDownClass(cls):7        cls.shared = None
Output
NameError: name 'unittest' is not defined

What to observe: Be careful with shared mutable state in class fixtures because tests can accidentally affect each other.

Example 3. Assertion helpers communicate the check

What this does and why: Framework-specific assertion methods produce better failure messages than hand-written if-statements.

1self.assertEqual(2 + 2, 4)2self.assertTrue(True)3self.assertRaises(ValueError)
Output
NameError: name 'self' is not defined

What to observe: Each assertion names the expectation directly, which makes failures easier to scan in test output.

Common Misconceptions

Misconception

Wrong assumption: unittest is only syntax sugar with no runtime model behind it.

1import unittest2 3class BankAccount:4    def __init__(self, balance=0):5        self.balance = balance6    def deposit(self, amount):7        self.balance += amount8        return self.balance9 10class BankAccountTests(unittest.TestCase):11    def setUp(self):12        self.account = BankAccount(100)13    def test_deposit(self):14        self.assertEqual(self.account.deposit(25), 125)15 16case = BankAccountTests()17case.setUp()18case.test_deposit()19print("passed")
Output
passed

Correction: Run the first example and watch the behavior directly. `setUp` runs before each test method, giving each test a fresh fixture.

Misconception

Wrong assumption: Once the common case for unittest works, the edge cases no longer matter.

1class DemoTests(unittest.TestCase):2    @classmethod3    def setUpClass(cls):4        cls.shared = [1, 2, 3]5    @classmethod6    def tearDownClass(cls):7        cls.shared = None
Output
NameError: name 'unittest' is not defined

Correction: The second example shows the tradeoff more clearly. Be careful with shared mutable state in class fixtures because tests can accidentally affect each other.

Trade-offs & When to Avoid

`unittest` is reliable and standard, but many teams prefer pytest because it removes more boilerplate while staying compatible with much of the same ecosystem.

Pitfalls & Gotchas

A common trap around unittest
1self.assertEqual(2 + 2, 4)2self.assertTrue(True)3self.assertRaises(ValueError)
Output
NameError: name 'self' is not defined

Root cause: The bug comes from assuming the shortest form is always the clearest or safest form. In Python, the runtime model still matters.

Fix: Each assertion names the expectation directly, which makes failures easier to scan in test output.

Self-Check Questions

What problem does unittest solve?
Tests exist so code changes can be checked automatically. `unittest` provides a standard structure that ships with Python itself.
What mental model should you picture for unittest?
How `TestCase` organizes tests, What `setUp`, `tearDown`, and class-level fixtures do, Why assertion methods communicate intent better than plain `assert` in this framework
When should you limit or avoid unittest?
`unittest` is reliable and standard, but many teams prefer pytest because it removes more boilerplate while staying compatible with much of the same ecosystem.
Back to Dependency TableNext concept → PY52
PY52 · Layer 12 · Testing

pytest

Read firstPY25PY35
Used later inPY54PY55PY56PY57PY58PY59

What you'll learn

  • Why pytest usually feels lighter than `unittest`
  • How fixtures and parametrization scale test setup
  • Where `conftest.py` and monkeypatching fit
Why This Matters
pytest exists because test code should be easy to write and easy to read. It removes ceremony so the test intent stays front and center.

Concept Explanation

pytest is a third-party testing framework that favors plain functions, powerful fixtures, and concise assertions.

pytest exists because test code should be easy to write and easy to read. It removes ceremony so the test intent stays front and center.

Builds on: PY25, PY35. Prepares you for: PY54, PY55, PY56.

Mental Model

pytest discovers test functions -> injects fixtures by name -> rewrites asserts for rich failure output

Worked Examples

Example 1. The same style without a test class

What this does and why: Plain functions are often enough, which makes tests feel closer to normal Python code.

1def deposit(balance, amount):2    return balance + amount3 4def test_deposit():5    assert deposit(100, 25) == 1256 7test_deposit()8print("passed")
Output
passed

What to observe: pytest rewrites `assert` to show rich comparison output on failure, so plain asserts become pleasant to use.

Example 2. A fixture and parametrized test

What this does and why: Fixtures centralize setup and parametrization multiplies cases cleanly.

1import pytest2 3@pytest.fixture4def base_balance():5    return 1006 7@pytest.mark.parametrize("amount, expected", [(25, 125), (0, 100)])8def test_deposit(base_balance, amount, expected):9    assert base_balance + amount == expected10 11for amount, expected in [(25, 125), (0, 100)]:12    test_deposit(base_balance(), amount, expected)13print("all cases passed")
Output
ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'pytest'

What to observe: This pattern scales far better than hand-copying almost identical tests.

Example 3. `conftest.py` and monkeypatch

What this does and why: Shared fixtures live in `conftest.py`, and `monkeypatch` lets tests temporarily replace behavior safely.

1import os2import pytest3 4def test_env(monkeypatch):5    monkeypatch.setenv("APP_MODE", "test")6    assert True7 8mp = pytest.MonkeyPatch()9test_env(mp)10print(os.environ["APP_MODE"])11mp.undo()
Output
ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'pytest'

What to observe: Monkeypatching is useful for environment variables, module attributes, and small controlled substitutions during tests.

Common Misconceptions

Misconception

Wrong assumption: pytest is only syntax sugar with no runtime model behind it.

1def deposit(balance, amount):2    return balance + amount3 4def test_deposit():5    assert deposit(100, 25) == 1256 7test_deposit()8print("passed")
Output
passed

Correction: Run the first example and watch the behavior directly. pytest rewrites `assert` to show rich comparison output on failure, so plain asserts become pleasant to use.

Misconception

Wrong assumption: Once the common case for pytest works, the edge cases no longer matter.

1import pytest2 3@pytest.fixture4def base_balance():5    return 1006 7@pytest.mark.parametrize("amount, expected", [(25, 125), (0, 100)])8def test_deposit(base_balance, amount, expected):9    assert base_balance + amount == expected10 11for amount, expected in [(25, 125), (0, 100)]:12    test_deposit(base_balance(), amount, expected)13print("all cases passed")
Output
ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'pytest'

Correction: The second example shows the tradeoff more clearly. This pattern scales far better than hand-copying almost identical tests.

Trade-offs & When to Avoid

pytest is usually the better default for new projects, but `unittest` knowledge still matters because the stdlib and older codebases use it heavily.

Pitfalls & Gotchas

A common trap around pytest
1import os2import pytest3 4def test_env(monkeypatch):5    monkeypatch.setenv("APP_MODE", "test")6    assert True7 8mp = pytest.MonkeyPatch()9test_env(mp)10print(os.environ["APP_MODE"])11mp.undo()
Output
ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'pytest'

Root cause: The bug comes from assuming the shortest form is always the clearest or safest form. In Python, the runtime model still matters.

Fix: Monkeypatching is useful for environment variables, module attributes, and small controlled substitutions during tests.

Self-Check Questions

What problem does pytest solve?
pytest exists because test code should be easy to write and easy to read. It removes ceremony so the test intent stays front and center.
What mental model should you picture for pytest?
Why pytest usually feels lighter than `unittest`, How fixtures and parametrization scale test setup, Where `conftest.py` and monkeypatching fit
When should you limit or avoid pytest?
pytest is usually the better default for new projects, but `unittest` knowledge still matters because the stdlib and older codebases use it heavily.
Back to Dependency TableNext concept → PY53
PY53 · Layer 12 · Testing

Mocking

Read firstPY25PY35
Used later inPY54PY55PY56PY57PY58PY59

What you'll learn

  • How `Mock`, `MagicMock`, and `patch` differ
  • Why patch location matters
  • How over-mocking can make tests meaningless
Why This Matters
Mocks exist because real networks, clocks, payment gateways, and file systems make tests slow, flaky, or expensive when the unit under test does not need the real dependency.

Concept Explanation

Mocking replaces collaborators with controllable doubles so a test can focus on one unit's behavior without touching real external systems.

Mocks exist because real networks, clocks, payment gateways, and file systems make tests slow, flaky, or expensive when the unit under test does not need the real dependency.

Builds on: PY25, PY35. Prepares you for: PY54, PY55, PY56.

Mental Model

unit under test -> mocked dependency returns controlled values -> assertions check calls and behavior

Worked Examples

Example 1. A basic mock object

What this does and why: Mocks record calls and return configured values.

1from unittest.mock import Mock2 3client = Mock()4client.fetch.return_value = {"status": 200}5print(client.fetch("/health"))6print(client.fetch.call_count)
Output
{'status': 200}
1

What to observe: You can assert both what was returned and how the mock was used.

Example 2. `patch` as a context manager

What this does and why: Patch where the code under test looks up the name, not where the original object was defined.

1from unittest.mock import patch2 3with patch("module_under_test.requests.get") as fake_get:4    fake_get.return_value.status_code = 200
Output
ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'module_under_test'

What to observe: Location matters because imports copy names into module namespaces.

Example 3. The over-mocking trap

What this does and why: If every collaborator is mocked, you may stop testing anything real about the system.

1from unittest.mock import Mock2 3# This test passes but proves nothing useful:4# every collaborator is mocked, so we are testing the mock's behaviour.\nservice = Mock()\nservice.calculate.return_value = 42\nresult = service.calculate(10, 20)\nassert result == 42   # trivially true - we set it ourselves\n\n# Better: keep pure business logic real and only mock I/O boundaries.\ndef calculate(a, b):\n    return a + b       # real logic - no mock needed\n\nassert calculate(10, 20) == 30\nprint("Pure logic test: real code, real assertion")\nprint("Mock the HTTP client, the DB driver - not your own math")\n
Output
Pure logic test: real code, real assertion
Mock the HTTP client, the DB driver - not your own math

What to observe: A test full of mocks can pass while the real pieces fail to fit together. Balance unit isolation with integration coverage.

Common Misconceptions

Misconception

Wrong assumption: Mocking is only syntax sugar with no runtime model behind it.

1from unittest.mock import Mock2 3client = Mock()4client.fetch.return_value = {"status": 200}5print(client.fetch("/health"))6print(client.fetch.call_count)
Output
{'status': 200}
1

Correction: Run the first example and watch the behavior directly. You can assert both what was returned and how the mock was used.

Misconception

Wrong assumption: Once the common case for Mocking works, the edge cases no longer matter.

1from unittest.mock import patch2 3with patch("module_under_test.requests.get") as fake_get:4    fake_get.return_value.status_code = 200
Output
ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'module_under_test'

Correction: The second example shows the tradeoff more clearly. Location matters because imports copy names into module namespaces.

Trade-offs & When to Avoid

Mock the parts that make the test slow or unstable, not every single dependency. Otherwise you end up testing your mocks instead of your code.

Pitfalls & Gotchas

A common trap around Mocking
1from unittest.mock import Mock2 3# This test passes but proves nothing useful:4# every collaborator is mocked, so we are testing the mock's behaviour.\nservice = Mock()\nservice.calculate.return_value = 42\nresult = service.calculate(10, 20)\nassert result == 42   # trivially true - we set it ourselves\n\n# Better: keep pure business logic real and only mock I/O boundaries.\ndef calculate(a, b):\n    return a + b       # real logic - no mock needed\n\nassert calculate(10, 20) == 30\nprint("Pure logic test: real code, real assertion")\nprint("Mock the HTTP client, the DB driver - not your own math")\n
Output
Pure logic test: real code, real assertion
Mock the HTTP client, the DB driver - not your own math

Root cause: The bug comes from assuming the shortest form is always the clearest or safest form. In Python, the runtime model still matters.

Fix: A test full of mocks can pass while the real pieces fail to fit together. Balance unit isolation with integration coverage.

Self-Check Questions

What problem does Mocking solve?
Mocks exist because real networks, clocks, payment gateways, and file systems make tests slow, flaky, or expensive when the unit under test does not need the real dependency.
What mental model should you picture for Mocking?
How `Mock`, `MagicMock`, and `patch` differ, Why patch location matters, How over-mocking can make tests meaningless
When should you limit or avoid Mocking?
Mock the parts that make the test slow or unstable, not every single dependency. Otherwise you end up testing your mocks instead of your code.
Back to Dependency TableNext concept → PY54
PY54 · Layer 13 · Performance & Internals

Profiling

Read firstPY01PY02PY03PY04PY05PY06PY07PY08PY09PY10PY11PY12PY13PY14PY15PY16PY17PY18PY19PY20PY21PY22PY23PY24PY25PY26PY27PY28PY29PY30PY31PY32PY33PY34PY35PY36PY37PY38PY39PY40PY41PY42PY43PY44PY45PY46PY47PY48PY49PY50PY51PY52PY53
Used later inPY57PY58PY59

What you'll learn

  • How `cProfile` summarizes call costs
  • What line-level profilers add
  • Why memory profiling matters separately from CPU time
Why This Matters
Performance work without measurement is mostly storytelling. Profilers turn vague suspicions into concrete hotspots.

Concept Explanation

Profiling measures where time or memory actually goes so you can optimize evidence, not guesses.

Performance work without measurement is mostly storytelling. Profilers turn vague suspicions into concrete hotspots.

Builds on: PY01, PY02, PY03, PY04, PY05, PY06, PY07, PY08, PY09, PY10, PY11, PY12, PY13, PY14, PY15, PY16, PY17, PY18, PY19, PY20, PY21, PY22, PY23, PY24, PY25, PY26, PY27, PY28, PY29, PY30, PY31, PY32, PY33, PY34, PY35, PY36, PY37, PY38, PY39, PY40, PY41, PY42, PY43, PY44, PY45, PY46, PY47, PY48, PY49, PY50, PY51, PY52, PY53. Prepares you for: PY57, PY58, PY59.

Mental Model

program run -> profiler records timing or memory -> report identifies hotspots

Worked Examples

Example 1. `cProfile` a slow function

What this does and why: Function-level profiling is the first pass for many problems.

1import cProfile2 3def slow():4    total = 05    for i in range(10000):6        total += i * i7    return total8 9cProfile.run("slow()")
Output
         3 function calls in 0.000 seconds

   Ordered by: standard name

   ncalls  tottime  percall  cumtime  percall filename:lineno(function)
        1    0.000    0.000    0.000    0.000 <string>:1(<module>)
        1    0.000    0.000    0.000    0.000 {built-in method builtins.exec}
        1    0.000    0.000    0.000    0.000 {method 'disable' of '_lsprof.Profiler' objects}


NameError: name 'slow' is not defined

What to observe: The report shows how many times each function was called and where cumulative time accumulated.

Example 2. Line profilers zoom in further

What this does and why: Once you know the slow function, line-level tools help find the expensive statement inside it.

1import timeit2 3# Simulate line-level comparison by timing two candidate implementations.4# (line_profiler is a third-party tool; timeit is the stdlib equivalent5#  for comparing individual expressions.)6 7stmt_concat =
Output
SyntaxError: invalid syntax (<snippet>, line 7)

What to observe: This is useful when one function contains both cheap and expensive branches.

Example 3. Memory deserves separate attention

What this does and why: CPU-fast code can still be memory-hungry.

1import tracemalloc2 3tracemalloc.start()4 5# Allocate a large list to create a measurable footprint.6snapshot_before = tracemalloc.take_snapshot()7big_list = list(range(100_000))8snapshot_after = tracemalloc.take_snapshot()9 10stats = snapshot_after.compare_to(snapshot_before, "lineno")11for stat in stats[:3]:12    print(stat)13 14del big_list15tracemalloc.stop()16print("tracemalloc is the stdlib memory profiler; memory_profiler is a popular third-party alternative")
Output
<snippet>:7: size=3898 KiB (+3898 KiB), count=99745 (+99745), average=40 B
C:\Users\Loki\AppData\Local\Programs\Python\Python312\Lib\tracemalloc.py:560: size=312 B (+312 B), count=2 (+2), average=156 B
C:\Users\Loki\AppData\Local\Programs\Python\Python312\Lib\tracemalloc.py:423: size=312 B (+312 B), count=2 (+2), average=156 B
tracemalloc is the stdlib memory profiler; memory_profiler is a popular third-party alternative

What to observe: Optimize the bottleneck you actually have: time, memory, or both.

Common Misconceptions

Misconception

Wrong assumption: Profiling is only syntax sugar with no runtime model behind it.

1import cProfile2 3def slow():4    total = 05    for i in range(10000):6        total += i * i7    return total8 9cProfile.run("slow()")
Output
         3 function calls in 0.000 seconds

   Ordered by: standard name

   ncalls  tottime  percall  cumtime  percall filename:lineno(function)
        1    0.000    0.000    0.000    0.000 <string>:1(<module>)
        1    0.000    0.000    0.000    0.000 {built-in method builtins.exec}
        1    0.000    0.000    0.000    0.000 {method 'disable' of '_lsprof.Profiler' objects}


NameError: name 'slow' is not defined

Correction: Run the first example and watch the behavior directly. The report shows how many times each function was called and where cumulative time accumulated.

Misconception

Wrong assumption: Once the common case for Profiling works, the edge cases no longer matter.

1import timeit2 3# Simulate line-level comparison by timing two candidate implementations.4# (line_profiler is a third-party tool; timeit is the stdlib equivalent5#  for comparing individual expressions.)6 7stmt_concat =
Output
SyntaxError: invalid syntax (<snippet>, line 7)

Correction: The second example shows the tradeoff more clearly. This is useful when one function contains both cheap and expensive branches.

Trade-offs & When to Avoid

Profile before optimizing and again after optimizing. Without the second measurement, you do not know whether the change actually helped.

Pitfalls & Gotchas

A common trap around Profiling
1import tracemalloc2 3tracemalloc.start()4 5# Allocate a large list to create a measurable footprint.6snapshot_before = tracemalloc.take_snapshot()7big_list = list(range(100_000))8snapshot_after = tracemalloc.take_snapshot()9 10stats = snapshot_after.compare_to(snapshot_before, "lineno")11for stat in stats[:3]:12    print(stat)13 14del big_list15tracemalloc.stop()16print("tracemalloc is the stdlib memory profiler; memory_profiler is a popular third-party alternative")
Output
<snippet>:7: size=3898 KiB (+3898 KiB), count=99745 (+99745), average=40 B
C:\Users\Loki\AppData\Local\Programs\Python\Python312\Lib\tracemalloc.py:560: size=312 B (+312 B), count=2 (+2), average=156 B
C:\Users\Loki\AppData\Local\Programs\Python\Python312\Lib\tracemalloc.py:423: size=312 B (+312 B), count=2 (+2), average=156 B
tracemalloc is the stdlib memory profiler; memory_profiler is a popular third-party alternative

Root cause: The bug comes from assuming the shortest form is always the clearest or safest form. In Python, the runtime model still matters.

Fix: Optimize the bottleneck you actually have: time, memory, or both.

Self-Check Questions

What problem does Profiling solve?
Performance work without measurement is mostly storytelling. Profilers turn vague suspicions into concrete hotspots.
What mental model should you picture for Profiling?
How `cProfile` summarizes call costs, What line-level profilers add, Why memory profiling matters separately from CPU time
When should you limit or avoid Profiling?
Profile before optimizing and again after optimizing. Without the second measurement, you do not know whether the change actually helped.
Back to Dependency TableNext concept → PY55
PY55 · Layer 13 · Performance & Internals

Python Memory Model Deep-Dive

Read firstPY01PY02PY03PY04PY05PY06PY07PY08PY09PY10PY11PY12PY13PY14PY15PY16PY17PY18PY19PY20PY21PY22PY23PY24PY25PY26PY27PY28PY29PY30PY31PY32PY33PY34PY35PY36PY37PY38PY39PY40PY41PY42PY43PY44PY45PY46PY47PY48PY49PY50PY51PY52PY53
Used later inPY57PY58PY59

What you'll learn

  • Why small integers and some strings may be interned
  • How reference counting and cyclic GC divide the work
  • Where `__slots__` can materially reduce memory usage
Why This Matters
Memory behavior leaks into performance and even semantics, especially around identity, caching, and object lifetime.

Concept Explanation

CPython combines reference counting, cyclic garbage collection, object caching, and object-layout choices such as `__slots__` to manage memory.

Memory behavior leaks into performance and even semantics, especially around identity, caching, and object lifetime.

Builds on: PY01, PY02, PY03, PY04, PY05, PY06, PY07, PY08, PY09, PY10, PY11, PY12, PY13, PY14, PY15, PY16, PY17, PY18, PY19, PY20, PY21, PY22, PY23, PY24, PY25, PY26, PY27, PY28, PY29, PY30, PY31, PY32, PY33, PY34, PY35, PY36, PY37, PY38, PY39, PY40, PY41, PY42, PY43, PY44, PY45, PY46, PY47, PY48, PY49, PY50, PY51, PY52, PY53. Prepares you for: PY57, PY58, PY59.

Mental Model

object allocation -> references increase/decrease -> refcount hits zero or cycle collector cleans unreachable graph

Worked Examples

Example 1. Small integers are often cached

What this does and why: This is why identity experiments with tiny integers can be misleading.

1a = 2562b = 2563print(a is b)
Output
True

What to observe: CPython commonly interns integers from -5 to 256. That is an implementation optimization, not a value-equality rule.

Example 2. Some strings are interned too

What this does and why: Short identifier-like strings are frequent candidates for interning.

1x = "hello"2y = "hello"3print(x is y)
Output
True

What to observe: This may print `True`, but you should still compare strings with `==` because interning is not the semantic rule.

Example 3. `__slots__` removes per-instance dictionaries

What this does and why: When you create huge numbers of simple objects, layout choices matter.

1class Plain:2    def __init__(self):3        self.x = 14        self.y = 25 6class Slotted:7    __slots__ = ("x", "y")8    def __init__(self):9        self.x = 110        self.y = 211 12print(hasattr(Plain(), "__dict__"))13print(hasattr(Slotted(), "__dict__"))
Output
True
False

What to observe: Slots trade flexibility for lower per-instance overhead, which can matter a lot at scale.

Common Misconceptions

Misconception

Wrong assumption: Python Memory Model Deep-Dive is only syntax sugar with no runtime model behind it.

1a = 2562b = 2563print(a is b)
Output
True

Correction: Run the first example and watch the behavior directly. CPython commonly interns integers from -5 to 256. That is an implementation optimization, not a value-equality rule.

Misconception

Wrong assumption: Once the common case for Python Memory Model Deep-Dive works, the edge cases no longer matter.

1x = "hello"2y = "hello"3print(x is y)
Output
True

Correction: The second example shows the tradeoff more clearly. This may print `True`, but you should still compare strings with `==` because interning is not the semantic rule.

Trade-offs & When to Avoid

Do not write correctness logic around caching or interning. Those are optimizations. Treat them as implementation details unless you are measuring performance.

Pitfalls & Gotchas

A common trap around Python Memory Model Deep-Dive
1class Plain:2    def __init__(self):3        self.x = 14        self.y = 25 6class Slotted:7    __slots__ = ("x", "y")8    def __init__(self):9        self.x = 110        self.y = 211 12print(hasattr(Plain(), "__dict__"))13print(hasattr(Slotted(), "__dict__"))
Output
True
False

Root cause: The bug comes from assuming the shortest form is always the clearest or safest form. In Python, the runtime model still matters.

Fix: Slots trade flexibility for lower per-instance overhead, which can matter a lot at scale.

Self-Check Questions

What problem does Python Memory Model Deep-Dive solve?
Memory behavior leaks into performance and even semantics, especially around identity, caching, and object lifetime.
What mental model should you picture for Python Memory Model Deep-Dive?
Why small integers and some strings may be interned, How reference counting and cyclic GC divide the work, Where `__slots__` can materially reduce memory usage
When should you limit or avoid Python Memory Model Deep-Dive?
Do not write correctness logic around caching or interning. Those are optimizations. Treat them as implementation details unless you are measuring performance.
Back to Dependency TableNext concept → PY56
PY56 · Layer 13 · Performance & Internals

Writing Faster Python

Read firstPY01PY02PY03PY04PY05PY06PY07PY08PY09PY10PY11PY12PY13PY14PY15PY16PY17PY18PY19PY20PY21PY22PY23PY24PY25PY26PY27PY28PY29PY30PY31PY32PY33PY34PY35PY36PY37PY38PY39PY40PY41PY42PY43PY44PY45PY46PY47PY48PY49PY50PY51PY52PY53
Used later inPY57PY58PY59

What you'll learn

  • Why O(n log n) beats clever O(nÂ²) code
  • How local lookups and comprehensions can help a little
  • When to move numeric heavy lifting to vectorized libraries
Why This Matters
Most performance wins come from changing the shape of the work, not shaving nanoseconds off the wrong loop.

Concept Explanation

Fast Python starts with algorithmic complexity, then applies measurements and data-structure choices before micro-optimizations.

Most performance wins come from changing the shape of the work, not shaving nanoseconds off the wrong loop.

Builds on: PY01, PY02, PY03, PY04, PY05, PY06, PY07, PY08, PY09, PY10, PY11, PY12, PY13, PY14, PY15, PY16, PY17, PY18, PY19, PY20, PY21, PY22, PY23, PY24, PY25, PY26, PY27, PY28, PY29, PY30, PY31, PY32, PY33, PY34, PY35, PY36, PY37, PY38, PY39, PY40, PY41, PY42, PY43, PY44, PY45, PY46, PY47, PY48, PY49, PY50, PY51, PY52, PY53. Prepares you for: PY57, PY58, PY59.

Mental Model

problem shape -> algorithm choice -> data structure choice -> only then small runtime tweaks

Worked Examples

Example 1. Algorithmic wins dominate

What this does and why: Changing complexity beats micro-tuning nearly every time.

1import time2 3data = list(range(2000, 0, -1))   # 2000 unsorted integers4 5# O(n^2): for each element, scan all targets linearly6def has_pair_n2(lst, target):7    for i in lst:8        for j in lst:9            if i + j == target:10                return True11    return False12 13# O(n log n): sort once then use a set for O(1) lookups14def has_pair_nlogn(lst, target):15    seen = set(lst)16    return any(target - x in seen for x in lst)17 18t0 = time.perf_counter()19has_pair_n2(data, 9999)20t_n2 = time.perf_counter() - t021 22t0 = time.perf_counter()23has_pair_nlogn(data, 9999)24t_nlogn = time.perf_counter() - t025 26print(f"O(n^2) :     {t_n2:.4f}s")27print(f"O(n log n):  {t_nlogn:.6f}s")28print(f"Speedup: {t_n2/t_nlogn:.0f}x  â€” algorithm beats micro-tuning every time")
Output
O(n^2) :     0.0757s
O(n log n):  0.000168s
Speedup: 450x  â€” algorithm beats micro-tuning every time

What to observe: If the algorithm is wrong, local tweaks only make the wrong approach fail slightly faster.

Example 2. Local lookup is usually faster than global lookup

What this does and why: Python resolves local names more cheaply than globals.

1import timeit2setup = "x = 1\n"3print(timeit.timeit("x + 1", setup=setup, number=1_000_000))
Output
0.010100300016347319

What to observe: This difference exists, but it matters far less than choosing the right algorithm and data structure.

Example 3. Vectorized libraries can change the game

What this does and why: For heavy numeric work, pushing loops into optimized native code often dwarfs pure-Python tweaks.

1import time, array, math2 3N = 100_0004data = list(range(N))5 6# Python loop: each element is a full Python object7t0 = time.perf_counter()8result_loop = [x * x for x in data]9t_loop = time.perf_counter() - t010 11# array module: contiguous C doubles, but still iterated from Python12arr = array.array('d', data)13t0 = time.perf_counter()14result_arr = array.array('d', (x * x for x in arr))15t_arr = time.perf_counter() - t016 17print(f"List comprehension : {t_loop:.4f}s")18print(f"array iteration    : {t_arr:.4f}s")19print("For heavier numeric work, NumPy pushes the loop into C,")20print("giving another 10-100x improvement over pure Python iteration.")
Output
List comprehension : 0.0025s
array iteration    : 0.0054s
For heavier numeric work, NumPy pushes the loop into C,
giving another 10-100x improvement over pure Python iteration.

What to observe: The best optimization is often moving the hot path to a library designed for that workload.

Common Misconceptions

Misconception

Wrong assumption: Writing Faster Python is only syntax sugar with no runtime model behind it.

1import time2 3data = list(range(2000, 0, -1))   # 2000 unsorted integers4 5# O(n^2): for each element, scan all targets linearly6def has_pair_n2(lst, target):7    for i in lst:8        for j in lst:9            if i + j == target:10                return True11    return False12 13# O(n log n): sort once then use a set for O(1) lookups14def has_pair_nlogn(lst, target):15    seen = set(lst)16    return any(target - x in seen for x in lst)17 18t0 = time.perf_counter()19has_pair_n2(data, 9999)20t_n2 = time.perf_counter() - t021 22t0 = time.perf_counter()23has_pair_nlogn(data, 9999)24t_nlogn = time.perf_counter() - t025 26print(f"O(n^2) :     {t_n2:.4f}s")27print(f"O(n log n):  {t_nlogn:.6f}s")28print(f"Speedup: {t_n2/t_nlogn:.0f}x  â€” algorithm beats micro-tuning every time")
Output
O(n^2) :     0.0757s
O(n log n):  0.000168s
Speedup: 450x  â€” algorithm beats micro-tuning every time

Correction: Run the first example and watch the behavior directly. If the algorithm is wrong, local tweaks only make the wrong approach fail slightly faster.

Misconception

Wrong assumption: Once the common case for Writing Faster Python works, the edge cases no longer matter.

1import timeit2setup = "x = 1\n"3print(timeit.timeit("x + 1", setup=setup, number=1_000_000))
Output
0.010100300016347319

Correction: The second example shows the tradeoff more clearly. This difference exists, but it matters far less than choosing the right algorithm and data structure.

Trade-offs & When to Avoid

Benchmark representative workloads, not tiny artificial fragments detached from the real program.

Pitfalls & Gotchas

A common trap around Writing Faster Python
1import time, array, math2 3N = 100_0004data = list(range(N))5 6# Python loop: each element is a full Python object7t0 = time.perf_counter()8result_loop = [x * x for x in data]9t_loop = time.perf_counter() - t010 11# array module: contiguous C doubles, but still iterated from Python12arr = array.array('d', data)13t0 = time.perf_counter()14result_arr = array.array('d', (x * x for x in arr))15t_arr = time.perf_counter() - t016 17print(f"List comprehension : {t_loop:.4f}s")18print(f"array iteration    : {t_arr:.4f}s")19print("For heavier numeric work, NumPy pushes the loop into C,")20print("giving another 10-100x improvement over pure Python iteration.")
Output
List comprehension : 0.0025s
array iteration    : 0.0054s
For heavier numeric work, NumPy pushes the loop into C,
giving another 10-100x improvement over pure Python iteration.

Root cause: The bug comes from assuming the shortest form is always the clearest or safest form. In Python, the runtime model still matters.

Fix: The best optimization is often moving the hot path to a library designed for that workload.

Self-Check Questions

What problem does Writing Faster Python solve?
Most performance wins come from changing the shape of the work, not shaving nanoseconds off the wrong loop.
What mental model should you picture for Writing Faster Python?
Why O(n log n) beats clever O(nÂ²) code, How local lookups and comprehensions can help a little, When to move numeric heavy lifting to vectorized libraries
When should you limit or avoid Writing Faster Python?
Benchmark representative workloads, not tiny artificial fragments detached from the real program.
Back to Dependency TableNext concept → PY57
PY57 · Layer 14 · Ecosystem & Advanced Patterns

Common Design Patterns in Python

Read firstPY01PY02PY03PY04PY05PY06PY07PY08PY09PY10PY11PY12PY13PY14PY15PY16PY17PY18PY19PY20PY21PY22PY23PY24PY25PY26PY27PY28PY29PY30PY31PY32PY33PY34PY35PY36PY37PY38PY39PY40PY41PY42PY43PY44PY45PY46PY47PY48PY49PY50PY51PY52PY53PY54PY55PY56
Used later inNo direct dependents listed

What you'll learn

  • How Pythonic implementations differ from textbook UML-heavy versions
  • Where singleton, factory, observer, strategy, and repository patterns show up
  • Why patterns are tools, not badges of sophistication
Why This Matters
Patterns matter because recurring design problems deserve shared vocabulary, but Python often solves them with fewer moving parts than more ceremony-heavy languages.

Concept Explanation

Python uses classic patterns too, but the most useful versions usually lean on functions, modules, and duck typing instead of heavy class hierarchies.

Patterns matter because recurring design problems deserve shared vocabulary, but Python often solves them with fewer moving parts than more ceremony-heavy languages.

Builds on: PY01, PY02, PY03, PY04, PY05, PY06, PY07, PY08, PY09, PY10, PY11, PY12, PY13, PY14, PY15, PY16, PY17, PY18, PY19, PY20, PY21, PY22, PY23, PY24, PY25, PY26, PY27, PY28, PY29, PY30, PY31, PY32, PY33, PY34, PY35, PY36, PY37, PY38, PY39, PY40, PY41, PY42, PY43, PY44, PY45, PY46, PY47, PY48, PY49, PY50, PY51, PY52, PY53, PY54, PY55, PY56. Unlocks later integrations across the reference.

Mental Model

problem pattern -> choose lightest Python construct that preserves intent

Worked Examples

Example 1. Singleton via module state

What this does and why: In Python, a module is already a natural singleton because imports cache one module object.

1settings = {"mode": "prod"}2print(settings)
Output
{'mode': 'prod'}

What to observe: You often do not need a class-based singleton at all. A module-level object is simpler and clearer.

Example 2. Factory maps names to classes

What this does and why: A dictionary can be the whole factory when the decision is straightforward.

1class JsonExporter: pass2class CsvExporter: pass3 4factory = {"json": JsonExporter, "csv": CsvExporter}5print(factory["json"]())
Output
<__main__.JsonExporter object at 0x0000021A202B6480>

What to observe: This is more Pythonic than a long switch-like object hierarchy for many simple cases.

Example 3. Strategy via functions

What this does and why: A strategy can just be a callable passed in from the outside.

1def discount_10(price):2    return price * 0.93 4def checkout(price, pricing_strategy):5    return pricing_strategy(price)6 7print(checkout(100, discount_10))
Output
90.0

What to observe: Python's first-class functions make some classic class-heavy patterns much lighter.

Common Misconceptions

Misconception

Wrong assumption: Common Design Patterns in Python is only syntax sugar with no runtime model behind it.

1settings = {"mode": "prod"}2print(settings)
Output
{'mode': 'prod'}

Correction: Run the first example and watch the behavior directly. You often do not need a class-based singleton at all. A module-level object is simpler and clearer.

Misconception

Wrong assumption: Once the common case for Common Design Patterns in Python works, the edge cases no longer matter.

1class JsonExporter: pass2class CsvExporter: pass3 4factory = {"json": JsonExporter, "csv": CsvExporter}5print(factory["json"]())
Output
<__main__.JsonExporter object at 0x0000021A202B6480>

Correction: The second example shows the tradeoff more clearly. This is more Pythonic than a long switch-like object hierarchy for many simple cases.

Trade-offs & When to Avoid

Use patterns to clarify design, not to impress yourself. If a plain function or dict solves the problem cleanly, that is the better pattern for Python.

Pitfalls & Gotchas

A common trap around Common Design Patterns in Python
1def discount_10(price):2    return price * 0.93 4def checkout(price, pricing_strategy):5    return pricing_strategy(price)6 7print(checkout(100, discount_10))
Output
90.0

Root cause: The bug comes from assuming the shortest form is always the clearest or safest form. In Python, the runtime model still matters.

Fix: Python's first-class functions make some classic class-heavy patterns much lighter.

Self-Check Questions

What problem does Common Design Patterns in Python solve?
Patterns matter because recurring design problems deserve shared vocabulary, but Python often solves them with fewer moving parts than more ceremony-heavy languages.
What mental model should you picture for Common Design Patterns in Python?
How Pythonic implementations differ from textbook UML-heavy versions, Where singleton, factory, observer, strategy, and repository patterns show up, Why patterns are tools, not badges of sophistication
When should you limit or avoid Common Design Patterns in Python?
Use patterns to clarify design, not to impress yourself. If a plain function or dict solves the problem cleanly, that is the better pattern for Python.
Back to Dependency TableNext concept → PY58
PY58 · Layer 14 · Ecosystem & Advanced Patterns

Python Standard Library Tour

Read firstPY01PY02PY03PY04PY05PY06PY07PY08PY09PY10PY11PY12PY13PY14PY15PY16PY17PY18PY19PY20PY21PY22PY23PY24PY25PY26PY27PY28PY29PY30PY31PY32PY33PY34PY35PY36PY37PY38PY39PY40PY41PY42PY43PY44PY45PY46PY47PY48PY49PY50PY51PY52PY53PY54PY55PY56
Used later inNo direct dependents listed

What you'll learn

  • Which modules solve common real-world problems out of the box
  • How `itertools`, `functools`, `contextlib`, `enum`, `typing`, and others complement the core language
  • Why the stdlib is one of Python's biggest strengths
Why This Matters
Python's batteries-included philosophy matters because many tasks are already solved well enough without reaching for another dependency first.

Concept Explanation

The standard library is large enough that knowing where to look is often more valuable than memorizing every function name.

Python's batteries-included philosophy matters because many tasks are already solved well enough without reaching for another dependency first.

Builds on: PY01, PY02, PY03, PY04, PY05, PY06, PY07, PY08, PY09, PY10, PY11, PY12, PY13, PY14, PY15, PY16, PY17, PY18, PY19, PY20, PY21, PY22, PY23, PY24, PY25, PY26, PY27, PY28, PY29, PY30, PY31, PY32, PY33, PY34, PY35, PY36, PY37, PY38, PY39, PY40, PY41, PY42, PY43, PY44, PY45, PY46, PY47, PY48, PY49, PY50, PY51, PY52, PY53, PY54, PY55, PY56. Unlocks later integrations across the reference.

Mental Model

core language + standard library modules -> practical toolbox for everyday work

Worked Examples

Example 1. A tiny `itertools` sampler

What this does and why: These tools are great for iterator-heavy code.

1from itertools import chain, combinations2print(list(chain([1, 2], [3])))3print(list(combinations([1, 2, 3], 2)))
Output
[1, 2, 3]
[(1, 2), (1, 3), (2, 3)]

What to observe: `itertools` turns many nested-loop and windowing tasks into small, composable expressions.

Example 2. `functools` and `enum` in everyday code

What this does and why: These modules often remove repetitive boilerplate.

1from enum import Enum, auto2from functools import cache3 4class State(Enum):5    READY = auto()6    DONE = auto()7 8@cache9def square(n):10    return n * n11print(State.READY, square(4))
Output
State.READY 16

What to observe: `Enum` gives named constants and `cache` memoizes pure function calls with no size limit.

Example 3. `contextlib` utilities

What this does and why: The library includes helpers for temporary output redirection and controlled exception suppression.

1from contextlib import suppress2 3with suppress(FileNotFoundError):4    open("missing.txt")5print("continued")
Output
continued

What to observe: This is safer and narrower than a bare `except` because it names the exact exception being tolerated.

Common Misconceptions

Misconception

Wrong assumption: Python Standard Library Tour is only syntax sugar with no runtime model behind it.

1from itertools import chain, combinations2print(list(chain([1, 2], [3])))3print(list(combinations([1, 2, 3], 2)))
Output
[1, 2, 3]
[(1, 2), (1, 3), (2, 3)]

Correction: Run the first example and watch the behavior directly. `itertools` turns many nested-loop and windowing tasks into small, composable expressions.

Misconception

Wrong assumption: Once the common case for Python Standard Library Tour works, the edge cases no longer matter.

1from enum import Enum, auto2from functools import cache3 4class State(Enum):5    READY = auto()6    DONE = auto()7 8@cache9def square(n):10    return n * n11print(State.READY, square(4))
Output
State.READY 16

Correction: The second example shows the tradeoff more clearly. `Enum` gives named constants and `cache` memoizes pure function calls with no size limit.

Trade-offs & When to Avoid

Before adding a dependency, check whether the standard library already solves the problem well enough. It often does.

Pitfalls & Gotchas

A common trap around Python Standard Library Tour
1from contextlib import suppress2 3with suppress(FileNotFoundError):4    open("missing.txt")5print("continued")
Output
continued

Root cause: The bug comes from assuming the shortest form is always the clearest or safest form. In Python, the runtime model still matters.

Fix: This is safer and narrower than a bare `except` because it names the exact exception being tolerated.

Self-Check Questions

What problem does Python Standard Library Tour solve?
Python's batteries-included philosophy matters because many tasks are already solved well enough without reaching for another dependency first.
What mental model should you picture for Python Standard Library Tour?
Which modules solve common real-world problems out of the box, How `itertools`, `functools`, `contextlib`, `enum`, `typing`, and others complement the core language, Why the stdlib is one of Python's biggest strengths
When should you limit or avoid Python Standard Library Tour?
Before adding a dependency, check whether the standard library already solves the problem well enough. It often does.
Back to Dependency TableNext concept → PY59
PY59 · Layer 14 · Ecosystem & Advanced Patterns

Python Anti-Patterns

Read firstPY01PY02PY03PY04PY05PY06PY07PY08PY09PY10PY11PY12PY13PY14PY15PY16PY17PY18PY19PY20PY21PY22PY23PY24PY25PY26PY27PY28PY29PY30PY31PY32PY33PY34PY35PY36PY37PY38PY39PY40PY41PY42PY43PY44PY45PY46PY47PY48PY49PY50PY51PY52PY53PY54PY55PY56
Used later inNo direct dependents listed

What you'll learn

  • Why mutable defaults, bare excepts, and `import *` keep hurting people
  • How `is None` and `isinstance` express the right questions
  • Why performance anti-patterns often start as innocent-looking loops
Why This Matters
Anti-patterns matter because avoiding a few traps saves disproportionate amounts of debugging time.

Concept Explanation

Many painful Python bugs come from a handful of recurring anti-patterns that feel convenient in the moment but blur the language's real semantics.

Anti-patterns matter because avoiding a few traps saves disproportionate amounts of debugging time.

Builds on: PY01, PY02, PY03, PY04, PY05, PY06, PY07, PY08, PY09, PY10, PY11, PY12, PY13, PY14, PY15, PY16, PY17, PY18, PY19, PY20, PY21, PY22, PY23, PY24, PY25, PY26, PY27, PY28, PY29, PY30, PY31, PY32, PY33, PY34, PY35, PY36, PY37, PY38, PY39, PY40, PY41, PY42, PY43, PY44, PY45, PY46, PY47, PY48, PY49, PY50, PY51, PY52, PY53, PY54, PY55, PY56. Unlocks later integrations across the reference.

Mental Model

tempting shortcut -> hidden semantic mismatch -> confusing bug later

Worked Examples

Example 1. Mutable defaults keep state between calls

What this does and why: This is one of Python's most common accidental bugs.

1def add(item, bucket=[]):2    bucket.append(item)3    return bucket4 5print(add(1))6print(add(2))
Output
[1]
[1, 2]

What to observe: The second call reuses the same list. Use `None` plus in-function initialization instead.

Example 2. Bare `except` catches too much

What this does and why: It can swallow `KeyboardInterrupt` and `SystemExit`, making programs harder to stop and debug.

1try:2    raise KeyboardInterrupt3except:4    print("too broad")
Output
too broad

What to observe: Catch the narrow exception you expect instead of erasing every possible signal.

Example 3. Ask the right question: identity versus type hierarchy

What this does and why: `is None` and `isinstance` express intent better than lookalikes.

1print(None is None)2print(type(True) == int)3print(isinstance(True, int))
Output
True
False
True

What to observe: Use `is None` for the singleton and `isinstance` when subclasses should count. Also avoid `import *` and repeated string concatenation in loops for the same clarity and performance reasons.

Common Misconceptions

Misconception

Wrong assumption: Python Anti-Patterns is only syntax sugar with no runtime model behind it.

1def add(item, bucket=[]):2    bucket.append(item)3    return bucket4 5print(add(1))6print(add(2))
Output
[1]
[1, 2]

Correction: Run the first example and watch the behavior directly. The second call reuses the same list. Use `None` plus in-function initialization instead.

Misconception

Wrong assumption: Once the common case for Python Anti-Patterns works, the edge cases no longer matter.

1try:2    raise KeyboardInterrupt3except:4    print("too broad")
Output
too broad

Correction: The second example shows the tradeoff more clearly. Catch the narrow exception you expect instead of erasing every possible signal.

Trade-offs & When to Avoid

Most anti-patterns come from fighting the language model instead of working with it. Learn the model once and many bugs disappear.

Pitfalls & Gotchas

A common trap around Python Anti-Patterns
1print(None is None)2print(type(True) == int)3print(isinstance(True, int))
Output
True
False
True

Root cause: The bug comes from assuming the shortest form is always the clearest or safest form. In Python, the runtime model still matters.

Fix: Use `is None` for the singleton and `isinstance` when subclasses should count. Also avoid `import *` and repeated string concatenation in loops for the same clarity and performance reasons.

Self-Check Questions

What problem does Python Anti-Patterns solve?
Anti-patterns matter because avoiding a few traps saves disproportionate amounts of debugging time.
What mental model should you picture for Python Anti-Patterns?
Why mutable defaults, bare excepts, and `import *` keep hurting people, How `is None` and `isinstance` express the right questions, Why performance anti-patterns often start as innocent-looking loops
When should you limit or avoid Python Anti-Patterns?
Most anti-patterns come from fighting the language model instead of working with it. Learn the model once and many bugs disappear.
Back to Dependency TableNext concept → quick-reference
What's Next?

Keep Going After This Reference

The official documentation stays authoritative for edge cases, the CPython source explains how the reference runtime behaves, and PEPs record the design reasoning behind major language changes. Once this document feels comfortable, the books below are excellent second passes because they focus on Pythonic design rather than raw syntax collection.

  • Official Python docs
  • CPython source on GitHub
  • PEPs index
  • Fluent Python by Luciano Ramalho
  • Python Cookbook by David Beazley and Brian K. Jones
Quick Reference

All 59 Concepts at a Glance

IDLayerConceptOne-line Description
PY010What is Python?Python is a high-level language whose reference runtime, CPython, compiles source code to bytecode and runs that bytecode on the Python Virtual Machine.
PY020Python Installation & the REPLInstalling Python gives you an interpreter, a launcher, and an interactive REPL where you can ask the runtime questions immediately.
PY030How Python Executes CodePython turns text into tokens, tokens into an abstract syntax tree, and that tree into bytecode that the virtual machine executes.
PY040Python VersioningPython's version story explains why Python 3 broke compatibility with Python 2 and why modern features usually arrive through the PEP process.
PY051Variables, Assignment, and Name BindingIn Python, assignment binds a name to an object; it does not copy a value into a box called a variable.
PY061Python's Type SystemPython is dynamically typed, strongly typed, and comfortable with duck typing: objects are judged by behavior more than declared ancestry.
PY071Built-in Data Types OverviewPython ships core numeric, text, binary, and singleton types because programs need stable building blocks before larger structures like lists and dictionaries make sense.
PY081OperatorsOperators are compact syntax for common operations, but each operator still follows precise runtime rules about precedence, identity, membership, and short-circuit evaluation.
PY092StringsStrings model human-readable text, which is not the same thing as raw bytes. They are immutable sequences of Unicode code points.
PY102ListsLists are mutable, ordered sequences that store references to objects rather than embedding the objects themselves.
PY112TuplesTuples are lightweight ordered sequences usually used for fixed-shape records, return values, and hashable grouping when their contents are immutable.
PY122DictionariesDictionaries map hashable keys to values and preserve insertion order in modern Python.
PY132Sets & FrozensetsSets store unique hashable items and make membership testing and set algebra concise and fast on average.
PY142The Collections Module`collections` provides specialized containers for patterns that plain lists and dicts can represent but do not represent elegantly.
PY153ConditionalsConditionals choose between code paths based on truthy and falsy values, and modern Python also supports structural pattern matching with `match/case` (added in Python 3.10).
PY163LoopsPython's `for` loop is really a foreach loop over an iterable, while `while` repeats until a condition becomes false.
PY173ComprehensionsComprehensions build lists, sets, and dicts from iterables with a compact transformation-and-filter syntax.
